summary: Primary evidence is the original document itself. Separate explanations clarify that each part of a multipart document, each counterpart, and each copy made by a uniform process constitute primary evidence, whereas copies of a common original do not.
ingredients: Primary evidence is the document itself produced for inspection.
Each part of a multipart document is primary evidence of the whole.
Each counterpart executed by a party is primary evidence against that party.
Copies made by a uniform process (printing, lithography, photography) are primary evidence of each other's contents.
Copies of a common original are not primary evidence of the original's contents.
explanation: Explanation 1.—Where a document is executed in several parts, each part is primary evidence of the document. Explanation 2.—Where a document is executed in counterpart, each counterpart being executed by one or some of the parties only, each counterpart is primary evidence as against the parties executing it. Explanation 3.—Where a number of documents are all made by one uniform process, as in the case of printing, lithography or photography, each is primary evidence of the contents of the rest; but, where they are all copies of a common original, they are not primary evidence of the contents of the original.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 57, Page 20

description: ( 2 ) g lawful control over the use of the computer or communication device; ( b ) during the said period, information of the kind contained in the electronic record or of the kind from which the information so contained is derived was regularly fed into the computer or communication device in the ordinary course of the said activities; ( c ) throughout the material part of the said period, the computer or communication device was operating properly or, if not, then in respect of any period in which it was not operating properly or was out of operation during that part of the period, was not such as to affect the electronic record or the accuracy of its contents; and ( d ) the information contained in the electronic record reproduces or is derived from such information fed into the computer or communication device in the ordinary course of the said activities. ( 3 ) Where over any period, the function of creating, storing or processing information for the purposes of any activity regularly carried on over that period as mentioned in clause ( a ) of sub-section ( 2 ) was regularly performed by means of one or more computers or communication device, whether— ( a ) in standalone mode; or ( b ) on a computer system; or ( c ) on a computer network; or ( d ) on a computer resource enabling information creation or providing information processing and storage; or ( e ) through an intermediary, all the computers or communication devices used for that purpose during that period shall be treated for the purposes of this section as constituting a single computer or communication device; and references in this section to a computer or communication device shall be construed accordingly. ( 4 ) In any proceeding where it is desired to give a statement in evidence by virtue of this section, a certificate doing any of the following things shall be submitted along with the electronic record at each instance where it is being submitted for admission, namely:— ( a ) identifying the electronic record containing the statement and describing the manner in which it was produced; ( b ) giving such particulars of any device involved in the production of that electronic record as may be appropriate for the purpose of showing that the electronic record was produced by a computer or a communication device referred to in clauses ( a ) to ( e ) of sub-section ( 3 ); ( c ) dealing with any of the matters to which the conditions mentioned in sub-section ( 2 ) relate, and purporting to be signed by a person in charge of the computer or communication device or the management of the relevant activities (whichever is appropriate) and an expert shall be evidence of any matter stated in the certificate; and for the purposes of this sub-section it shall be sufficient for a matter to be stated to the best of the knowledge and belief of the person stating it in the certificate specified in the Schedule. ( 5 ) For the purposes of this section,— ( a ) information shall be taken to be supplied to a computer or communication device if it is supplied thereto in any appropriate form and whether it is so supplied directly or (with or without human intervention) by means of any appropriate equipment; ( b ) a computer output shall be taken to have been produced by a computer or communication device whether it was produced by it directly or (with or without human intervention) by means of any appropriate equipment or by other electronic means as referred to in clauses ( a ) to ( e ) of sub-section ( 3 ).
summary: Section 63 sets out the conditions for admitting electronic records as evidence. It requires lawful control of the device, regular feeding of information, proper functioning of the device, and that the record accurately reflects the fed information. A certificate detailing the record, the device, and compliance with the conditions must accompany the electronic evidence.
ingredients: Lawful control over the computer or communication device (clause a).
Information regularly fed into the device during the period (clause b).
Device operating properly throughout the material period (clause c).
Record reproduces or is derived from the fed information (clause d).
Multiple devices used for the same activity are treated as a single device (sub‑section 3).
Certificate must identify the record and describe its production (sub‑section 4 a).
Certificate must give particulars of the device used (sub‑section 4 b).
Certificate must address conditions of sub‑section 2 and be signed by the person in charge (sub‑section 4 c).
Definition of information supplied to a device and computer output (sub‑section 5).
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 63, Page 22

description: 64.  Secondary evidence of the contents of the documents referred to in clause ( a ) of section 60, shall not be given unless the party proposing to give such secondary evidence has previously given to the party in whose possession or power the document is, or to his advocate or representative, such notice to produce it as is prescribed by law; and if no notice is prescribed by law, then such notice as the Court considers reasonable under the circumstances of the case: Provided that such notice shall not be required in order to render secondary evidence admissible in any of the following cases, or in any other case in which the Court thinks fit to dispense with it:— ( a ) when the document to be proved is itself a notice; ( b ) when, from the nature of the case, the adverse party must know that he will be required to produce it; ( c ) when it appears or is proved that the adverse party has obtained possession of the original by fraud or force; ( d ) when the adverse party or his agent has the original in Court; ( e ) when the adverse party or his agent has admitted the loss of the document; ( f ) when the person in possession of the document is out of reach of, or not subject to, the process of the Court.
summary: Section 64 governs the admissibility of secondary evidence of documents. It mandates that notice must be given to the holder of the original document before secondary evidence is admitted, except in specified situations where notice is waived. The listed exceptions include cases where the document is a notice, fraud, loss admission, or the holder is beyond the court's process.
ingredients: Notice to produce the original must be given before secondary evidence is admitted.
If no statutory notice is prescribed, the court may require reasonable notice.
Notice is not required in the listed exceptional circumstances.
exceptions: when the document to be proved is itself a notice
when, from the nature of the case, the adverse party must know that he will be required to produce it
when it appears or is proved that the adverse party has obtained possession of the original by fraud or force
when the adverse party or his agent has the original in Court
when the adverse party or his agent has admitted the loss of the document
when the person in possession of the document is out of reach of, or not subject to, the process of the Court
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 64, Page 24

description: 65.  If a document is alleged to be signed or to have been written wholly or in part by any person, the signature or the handwriting of so much of the document as is alleged to be in that person's handwriting must be proved to be in his handwriting.
summary: Section 65 requires that when a document is claimed to be signed or written by a person, the signature or the portion of handwriting alleged to be that person's must be proved to be in that person's handwriting.
ingredients: Proof must be produced that the signature is that of the alleged signer.
Proof must be produced that any alleged handwriting is that of the alleged writer.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 65, Page 24

description: 66.  Except in the case of a secure electronic signature, if the electronic signature of any subscriber is alleged to have been affixed to an electronic record, the fact that such electronic signature is the electronic signature of the subscriber must be proved.
summary: Section 66 mandates that, unless a secure electronic signature is used, the party alleging an electronic signature must prove that the signature indeed belongs to the subscriber.
ingredients: Proof that the alleged electronic signature belongs to the subscriber is required.
The requirement does not apply where a secure electronic signature is used.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 66, Page 24

description: 67.  If a document is required by law to be attested, it shall not be used as evidence until one attesting witness at least has been called for the purpose of proving its execution, if there be an attesting witness alive, and subject to the process of the Court and capable of giving evidence: Provided that it shall not be necessary to call an attesting witness in proof of the execution of any document, not being a will, which has been registered in accordance with the provisions of the Indian Registration Act, 1908, unless its execution by the person by whom it purports to have been executed is specifically denied.
summary: Section 67 states that a document which must be attested cannot be used as evidence unless at least one living attesting witness is called to prove its execution, except for registered non‑will documents where the witness is not required unless execution is denied.
ingredients: At least one living attesting witness must be called to prove execution of an attested document.
The witness must be capable of giving evidence and subject to court process.
For registered non‑will documents, an attesting witness is not required unless execution is specifically denied.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 67, Page 24

description: 68.  If no such attesting witness can be found, it must be proved that the attesting witness at least is in his handwriting, and that the signature of the person executing the document is in the handwriting of that person. Rules as to notice to produce. Proof of signature and handwriting of person alleged to have signed or written document produced. Proof as to electronic signature. Proof of execution of document required by law to be attested. Proof where no attesting witness found.
summary: Section 68 provides a fallback when an attesting witness cannot be located: the party must prove the handwriting of the alleged witness and the signature of the document's executor. It also summarises related evidentiary rules on notice, signatures, and electronic signatures.
ingredients: If no attesting witness is available, proof of the witness's handwriting is required.
Proof that the executor's signature is in his own handwriting is also required.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 68, Page 24

title: Admission of execution by party to attested document
description: The admission of a party to an attested document of its execution by himself shall be sufficient proof of its execution as against him, though it be a document required by law to be attested.
summary: If a party admits that he executed an attested document, that admission alone proves the execution against him, even if the document is one that the law requires to be attested.
ingredients: Admission by the party to execution of an attested document
Admission is sufficient proof against the party
Applies even if the document is required by law to be attested
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 69, Page 25

title: Proof when attesting witness denies execution
description: If the attesting witness denies or does not recollect the execution of the document, its execution may be proved by other evidence.
summary: When the witness who attested a document either denies or cannot recall the execution, the court may admit other evidence to prove that the document was executed.
ingredients: Attesting witness denial or lack of recollection
Court may admit alternative evidence to prove execution
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 70, Page 25

description: 71.  An attested document not required by law to be attested may be proved as if it was unattested.
summary: A document that has been attested but is not legally required to be attested can be treated as if it were unattested for the purpose of proof.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 71, Page 25

title: Comparison of signature, writing or seal; finger impressions
description: ( 1 ) In order to ascertain whether a signature, writing or seal is that of the person by whom it purports to have been written or made, any signature, writing, or seal admitted or proved to the satisfaction of the Court to have been written or made by that person may be compared with the one which is to be proved, although that signature, writing or seal has not been produced or proved for any other purpose. ( 2 ) The Court may direct any person present in Court to write any words or figures for the purpose of enabling the Court to compare the words or figures so written with any words or figures alleged to have been written by such person. ( 3 ) This section applies also, with any necessary modifications, to finger impressions.
summary: The court can compare a disputed signature, writing or seal with a known specimen to verify authenticity, and may order a person to write words or figures for comparison. The provision also extends, with modifications, to finger impressions.
ingredients: Comparison of disputed signature/writing/seal with a known specimen
Specimen may be admitted or proved to the court’s satisfaction
Court may direct a person to write words or figures for comparison
Provision applies, with modifications, to finger impressions
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 72, Page 25

title: Verification of digital signature
description: In order to ascertain whether a digital signature is that of the person by whom it purports to have been affixed, the Court may direct— ( a ) that person or the Controller or the Certifying Authority to produce the Digital Signature Certificate; ( b ) any other person to apply the public key listed in the Digital Signature Certificate and verify the digital signature purported to have been affixed by that person.
summary: To verify a digital signature, the court can order the person, the Controller, or the Certifying Authority to produce the Digital Signature Certificate, and can also direct another person to use the public key to verify the signature.
ingredients: Court may direct production of the Digital Signature Certificate by the person, Controller, or Certifying Authority
Court may direct a third party to apply the public key from the certificate to verify the digital signature
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 73, Page 25

title: Public and private documents
description: ( 1 ) The following documents are public documents:— ( a ) documents forming the acts, or records of the acts— ( i ) of the sovereign authority; ( ii ) of official bodies and tribunals; and ( iii ) of public officers, legislative, judicial and executive of India or of a foreign country; ( b ) public records kept in any State or Union territory of private documents. ( 2 ) All other documents except the documents referred to in sub-section ( 1 ) are private.
summary: Section 74 defines public documents as those forming acts or records of sovereign authorities, official bodies, tribunals, or public officers, as well as public records of private documents kept by a State or Union territory. All other documents are deemed private.
ingredients: Public documents include acts or records of sovereign authority, official bodies, tribunals, and public officers
Public records of private documents kept by any State or Union territory are also public
All documents not falling under sub‑section (1) are classified as private
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 74, Page 25

title: Certified copies of public documents
description: Every public officer having the custody of a public document, which any person has a right to inspect, shall give that person on demand a copy of it on payment of the legal fees therefor, together with a certificate written at the foot of such copy that it is a true copy of such document or part thereof, as the case may be, and such certificate shall be dated and subscribed by such officer with his name and his official title, and shall be sealed, whenever such officer is authorised by law to make use of a seal; and such copies so certified shall be called certified copies.
summary: A public officer who holds a public document must, upon request and payment of fees, provide a copy certified as a true copy, signed, dated, and sealed as required, and such copies are termed certified copies.
ingredients: Public officer must provide a copy on demand with payment of legal fees
Copy must include a certificate stating it is a true copy
Certificate must be dated, signed, and sealed as per authority
Certified copies are defined as such copies
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 75, Page 25

title: Use of certified copies as proof
description: Such certified copies may be produced in proof of the contents of the public documents or parts of the public documents of which they purport to be copies.
summary: Certified copies can be used as evidence to prove the contents of the original public documents or the portions they represent.
ingredients: Certified copies are admissible to prove contents of the original public document
Applies to whole documents or parts thereof
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 76, Page 26

title: Proof of various classes of public documents
description: The following public documents may be proved as follows:— ( a )Acts, orders or notifications of the Central Government in any of its Ministries and Departments or of any State Government or any Department of any State Government or Union territoryAdministration— ( i ) by the records of the Departments, certified by the head of those Departments respectively; or ( ii ) by any document purporting to be printed by order of any such Government; ( b ) the proceedings of Parliament or a State Legislature, by the journals of those bodies respectively, or by published Acts or abstracts, or by copies purporting to be printed by order of the Government concerned; ( c ) proclamations, orders or Regulations issued by the President of India or the Governor of a State or theAdministrator or Lieutenant Governor of a Union territory, by copies or extracts contained in the Official Gazette; ( d ) the Acts of the Executive or the proceedings of the Legislature of a foreign country, by journals published by their authority, or commonly received in that country as such, or by a copy certified under the seal of the country or sovereign, or by a recognition thereof in any Central Act; ( e ) the proceedings of a municipal or local body in a State, by a copy of such proceedings, certified by the legal keeper thereof, or by a printed book purporting to be published by the authority of such body; ( f ) public documents of any other class in a foreign country, by the original or by a copy certified by the legal keeper thereof, with a certificate under the seal of a Notary Public, or of an Indian Consul or diplomatic agent, that the copy is duly certified by the officer having the legal custody of the original, and upon proof of the character of the document according to the law of the foreign country.
summary: Section 77 enumerates the methods by which various categories of public documents—such as government acts, legislative proceedings, official gazette extracts, foreign official documents, and municipal records—may be proved in court, typically through certified records, official journals, or certified copies.
ingredients: Acts, orders, notifications proved by departmental records certified by heads or by printed documents
Parliamentary or state legislative proceedings proved by official journals, published Acts, or government‑ordered copies
Proclamations, orders, regulations proved by Official Gazette extracts
Foreign executive or legislative acts proved by official journals, certified copies, or recognition in Central Acts
Municipal or local body proceedings proved by certified copies or printed books
Other foreign public documents proved by original or certified copies with notarized or consular certification
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 77, Page 26

title: Presumptions as to documents
description: ( 1 ) The Court shall presume to be genuine every document purporting to be a certificate, certified copy or other document, which is by law declared to be admissible as evidence of any particular fact and which purports to be duly certified by any officer of the Central Government or of a State Government: Provided that such document is substantially in the form and purports to be executed in the manner directed by law in that behalf. ( 2 ) The Court shall also presume that any officer by whom any such document purports to be signed or certified, held, when he signed it, the official character which he claims in such paper.
summary: The court presumes documents that are declared admissible and appear to be duly certified by a central or state officer to be genuine, provided they follow the prescribed form. It also presumes that the officer who signed or certified the document possessed the official character claimed at the time of signing.
ingredients: Presumption of genuineness for certificates, certified copies, or other documents declared admissible
Presumption applies when the document appears to be duly certified by a central or state officer
Requirement that the document conforms substantially to the legally prescribed form
Presumption that the certifying officer held the official character claimed at the time of signing
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 78, Page 26

title: Documents produced as record of evidence, statements or confessions
description: Whenever any document is produced before any Court, purporting to be a record or memorandum of the evidence, or of any part of the evidence, given by a witness in a judicial proceeding or before any officer authorised by law to take such evidence or to be a statement or confession by any prisoner or accused person, taken in accordance with law,
summary: Section 79 deals with the admissibility of documents that claim to be records, memoranda, statements, or confessions taken in accordance with law, when such documents are produced before a court.
ingredients: Document purporting to be a record or memorandum of evidence
Document may represent a statement or confession by a prisoner or accused
Document must have been taken in accordance with law
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 79, Page 26

title: Presumption of genuineness of documents and statements
description: The Court shall presume that— ( i ) the document is genuine; ( ii ) any statements as to the circumstances under which it was taken, purporting to be made by the person signing it, are true; and ( iii ) such evidence, statement or confession was duly taken.
summary: Section 80 creates a presumption that a document is genuine, that statements about its taking are true, and that any confession or statement was properly taken, unless the contrary is proved. This shifts the evidential burden to the party challenging the document. It applies to all documents presented in court.
ingredients: Presumption of document genuineness
Presumption that statements about circumstances are true
Presumption that confessions or statements were duly taken
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 80, Page 27

title: Presumption of genuineness of official gazettes, newspapers, journals and statutory records
description: The Court shall presume the genuineness of every document purporting to be the Official Gazette, or to be a newspaper or journal, and of every document purporting to be a document directed by any law to be kept by any person, if such document is kept substantially in the form required by law and is produced from proper custody. Explanation. —For the purposes of this section and section 92, document is said to be in proper custody if it is in the place in which, and looked after by the person with whom such document is required to be kept; but no custody is improper if it is proved to have had a legitimate origin, or if the circumstances of the particular case are such as to render that origin probable.
summary: Section 81 presumes that official gazettes, newspapers, journals and statutory records are genuine when kept in the form required by law and produced from proper custody. Proper custody is defined as the document being in the place and under the care of the person legally required to keep it, unless its origin is proven illegitimate. The presumption can be rebutted with contrary evidence.
ingredients: Presumption of genuineness for official gazettes, newspapers, journals
Requirement that the document be kept substantially in the form required by law
Requirement that the document be produced from proper custody
Definition of proper custody
explanation: For the purposes of this section and section 92, document is said to be in proper custody if it is in the place in which, and looked after by the person with whom such document is required to be kept; but no custody is improper if it is proved to have had a legitimate origin, or if the circumstances of the particular case are such as to render that origin probable.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 81, Page 27

title: Presumption as to maps or plans made by Government authority
description: The Court shall presume that maps or plans purporting to be made by the authority of the Central Government or any State Government were so made, and are accurate; but maps or plans made for the purposes of any cause must be proved to be accurate.
summary: Section 82 presumes that maps or plans issued by the Central or State Government are accurate. However, if a map or plan is created for a specific cause, its accuracy must be proved. The presumption does not apply to privately prepared maps.
ingredients: Presumption of authenticity for government-issued maps or plans
Presumption of accuracy for such maps or plans
Requirement to prove accuracy for maps/plans made for a specific cause
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 82, Page 27

title: Presumption of genuineness of foreign government publications
description: The Court shall presume the genuineness of, every book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of any country, and to contain any of the laws of that country, and of every book purporting to contain reports of decisions of the Courts of such country.
summary: Section 83 creates a presumption that books published under the authority of a foreign government, containing that country's laws or court decisions, are genuine. This aids in admitting foreign legal materials without detailed authentication. The presumption can be challenged with evidence to the contrary.
ingredients: Presumption of genuineness for books printed/published under foreign government authority
Presumption for books containing foreign laws
Presumption for books containing foreign court decisions
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 83, Page 27

title: Presumption as to power-of-attorney documents
description: The Court shall presume that every document purporting to be a power-of-attorney, and to have been executed before, and authenticated by, a Notary Public, or any Court, Judge, Magistrate, Indian Consul or Vice-Consul, or representative of the Central Government, was so executed and authenticated.
summary: Section 84 presumes that a power-of-attorney document, if it claims to have been executed before and authenticated by a Notary Public, court officer, consul, or Central Government representative, is indeed so executed and authenticated. The presumption stands unless evidence disproves it.
ingredients: Presumption of execution before authorized persons
Presumption of authentication by Notary Public, court officers, consuls, or Central Government representatives
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 84, Page 27

title: Presumption as to electronic agreements with digital signatures
description: The Court shall presume that every electronic record purporting to be an agreement containing the electronic or digital signature of the parties was so concluded by affixing the electronic or digital signature of the parties.
summary: Section 85 presumes that an electronic agreement that shows the parties' electronic or digital signatures was indeed concluded by those parties using their signatures. The presumption can be rebutted with proof of forgery or lack of consent.
ingredients: Presumption that an electronic agreement with digital signatures was concluded by the parties
Reliance on the presence of electronic/digital signatures
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 85, Page 27

title: Presumptions relating to secure electronic records and signatures
description: ( 1 ) In any proceeding involving a secure electronic record, the Court shall presume unless contrary is proved, that the secure electronic record has not been altered since the specific point of time to which the secure status relates. ( 2 ) In any proceeding, involving secure electronic signature, the Court shall presume unless the contrary is proved that— ( a ) the secure electronic signature is affixed by subscriber with the intention of signing or approving the electronic record; ( b ) except in the case of a secure electronic record or a secure electronic signature, nothing in this section shall create any presumption, relating to authenticity and integrity of the electronic record or any electronic signature.
summary: Section 86 provides two presumptions: (1) a secure electronic record is presumed unaltered since the time of its secure status, and (2) a secure electronic signature is presumed to have been affixed by the subscriber with intent to sign, unless contradicted. The section clarifies that no other presumptions about authenticity or integrity arise from this provision.
ingredients: Presumption that a secure electronic record is unaltered since its secure point in time
Presumption that a secure electronic signature was affixed by the subscriber with intent
Limitation that no other presumptions about authenticity or integrity are created
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 86, Page 27

title: Presumption as to information in Electronic Signature Certificates
description: The Court shall presume, unless contrary is proved, that the information listed in an Electronic Signature Certificate is correct, except for information specified as subscriber information which has not been verified, if the certificate was accepted by the subscriber.
summary: Section 87 presumes that the details in an Electronic Signature Certificate are correct, except for unverified subscriber information, provided the subscriber has accepted the certificate. This presumption can be rebutted with evidence of inaccuracy.
ingredients: Presumption of correctness of information in an Electronic Signature Certificate
Exception for unverified subscriber information
Condition that the certificate must be accepted by the subscriber
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 87, Page 28

title: Presumption as to certified copies of foreign judicial records
description: ( 1 ) The Court may presume that any document purporting to be a certified copy of any judicial record of any country beyond India is genuine and accurate, if the document purports to be certified in any manner which is certified by any representative of the Central Government in or for such country to be the manner commonly in use in that country for the certification of copies of judicial records. ( 2 ) An officer who, with respect to any territory or place outside India is a Political Agent therefor, as defined in clause ( 43 ) of section 3 of the General Clauses Act, 1897, shall, for the purposes of this section, be deemed to be a representative of the Central Government in and for the country comprising that territory or place.
summary: Section 88 allows the court to presume that a certified copy of a foreign judicial record is genuine and accurate when it is certified in the manner commonly used in that country and certified by a Central Government representative. It also treats Political Agents abroad as Central Government representatives for this purpose.
ingredients: Presumption of genuineness and accuracy for certified foreign judicial records
Requirement that certification follows the common practice of the foreign country
Recognition of Central Government representatives and Political Agents as certifying authorities
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 88, Page 28

title: Presumption as to books, maps or charts used for public interest
description: The Court may presume that any book to which it may refer for information on matters of public or general interest, and that any published map or chart, the statements of which are relevant facts, and which is produced for its inspection, was written and published by the person, and at the time and place, by whom or at which it purports to have been written or published.
summary: Section 89 presumes that books, maps, or charts presented for public or general interest, and containing relevant factual statements, were authored and published by the persons claimed, at the claimed time and place. This aids in admitting such materials without further proof of authorship.
ingredients: Presumption of authorship and publication for books, maps, charts used for public interest
Condition that the material is produced for inspection
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 89, Page 28

title: Presumption as to electronic messages forwarded through mail servers
description: The Court may presume that an electronic message, forwarded by the originator through an electronic mail server to the addressee to whom the message purports to be addressed corresponds with the message as fed into his computer for transmission; but the Court shall not make any presumption as to the person by whom such message was sent.
summary: Section 90 presumes that an electronic mail message forwarded by the originator corresponds to the content entered into the sender's computer, but it does not presume the identity of the sender. The presumption assists in establishing the integrity of the message content.
ingredients: Presumption that forwarded electronic message matches the content entered by the originator
No presumption regarding the identity of the sender
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 90, Page 28

title: Presumption as to documents not produced after notice
description: The Court shall presume that every document, called for and not produced after notice to produce, was attested, stamped and executed in the manner required by law.
summary: Section 91 presumes that a document which has been ordered to be produced but is not produced was nonetheless attested, stamped, and executed as required by law, unless evidence shows otherwise. This presumption helps in cases of non-production.
ingredients: Presumption of proper attestation, stamping, and execution for documents not produced after notice
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 91, Page 28

title: Presumption as to old documents and signatures
description: Where any document, purporting or proved to be thirty years old, is produced from any custody which the Court in the particular case considers proper, the Court may presume that the signature and every other part of such document, which purports to be in the handwriting of any particular person, is in that person's handwriting, and, in the case of a document executed or attested, that it was duly executed and attested by the persons by whom it purports to be executed and attested. Explanation. —The Explanation to section 80 shall also apply to this section. Illustrations. ( a ) A has been in possession of landed property for a long time. He produces from his custody deeds relating to the land showing his titles to it. The custody shall be proper. ( b ) A produces deeds relating to landed property of which he is the mortgagee. The mortgagor is in possession. The custody shall be proper. ( c ) A, a connection of B, produces deeds relating to lands in B's possession, which were deposited with him by B for safe custody. The custody shall be proper.
summary: Section 92 allows the court to presume that a document up to thirty years old, produced from proper custody, bears the genuine handwriting and signatures of the persons claimed, and that it was duly executed and attested. The explanation of section 80 applies, and illustrations clarify the concept of proper custody.
ingredients: Presumption of authenticity for documents up to thirty years old
Requirement that the document be produced from proper custody
Presumption that signatures and handwriting are genuine
Presumption of proper execution and attestation
explanation: The Explanation to section 80 shall also apply to this section. Illustrations. ( a ) A has been in possession of landed property for a long time. He produces from his custody deeds relating to the land showing his titles to it. The custody shall be proper. ( b ) A produces deeds relating to landed property of which he is the mortgagee. The mortgagor is in possession. The custody shall be proper. ( c ) A, a connection of B, produces deeds relating to lands in B's possession, which were deposited with him by B for safe custody. The custody shall be proper.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 92, Page 28

title: Presumption as to electronic signatures on older electronic records
description: Where any electronic record, purporting or proved to be five years old, is produced from any custody which the Court in the particular case considers proper, the Court may presume that the electronic signature which purports to be the electronic signature of any particular person was so affixed by him or any person authorised by him in thi
summary: Section 93 provides a presumption that an electronic signature on an electronic record up to five years old, produced from proper custody, was affixed by the person or an authorized person, subject to the extent of the text provided. The presumption can be rebutted with contrary evidence.
ingredients: Presumption of authenticity for electronic signatures on records up to five years old
Requirement that the record be produced from proper custody
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 93, Page 28

description: When the terms of a contract, or of a grant, or of any other disposition of property, have been reduced to the form of a document, and in all cases in which any matter is required by law to be reduced to the form of a document, no evidence shall be given in proof of the terms of such contract, grant or other disposition of property, or of such matter, except the document itself, or secondary evidence of its contents in cases in which secondary evidence is admissible under the provisions hereinbefore contained. Exception 1.—When a public officer is required by law to be appointed in writing, and when it is shown that any particular person has acted as such officer, the writing by which he is appointed need not be proved. Exception 2.—Wills admitted to probate in India may be proved by the probate.
summary: Section 94 mandates that the written document is the primary evidence of its terms, and oral evidence is excluded unless secondary evidence is permissible. It provides two specific exceptions: appointment letters for public officers and probate of wills.
ingredients: Primary evidence: the document itself proves its terms.
Secondary evidence is admissible only when permitted by earlier provisions.
Exception 1: appointment letters for public officers need not be proved.
Exception 2: wills admitted to probate may be proved by the probate.
explanation:
Explanation 1.—This section applies equally to cases in which the contracts, grants or dispositions of property referred to are contained in one document, and to cases in which they are contained in more documents than one.
Explanation 2.—Where there are more originals than one, one original only need be proved.
Explanation 3.—The statement, in any document whatever, of a fact other than the facts referred to in this section, shall not preclude the admission of oral evidence as to the same fact.
Illustrations.
(a) If a contract be contained in several letters, all the letters in which it is contained must be proved.
(b) If a contract is contained in a bill of exchange, the bill of exchange must be proved.
(c) If a bill of exchange is drawn in a set of three, one only need be proved.
(d) A contracts, in writing, with B, for the delivery of indigo upon certain terms. The contract mentions the fact that B had paid the price of other indigo contracted for verbally on another occasion. Oral evidence is offered that no payment was made for the other indigo. The evidence is admissible.
(e) A gives B a receipt for money paid by B. Oral evidence is offered of the payment. The evidence is admissible.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 94, Page 29

description: When the terms of any such contract, grant or other disposition of property, or any matter required by law to be reduced to the form of a document, have been proved according to section 94, no evidence of any oral agreement or statement shall be admitted, as between the parties to any such instrument or their representatives in interest, for the purpose of contradicting, varying, adding to, or subtracting from, its terms: Provided that any fact may be proved which would invalidate any document, or which would entitle any person to any decree or order relating thereto; such as fraud, intimidation, illegality, want of due execution, want of capacity in any contracting party, want or failure of consideration, or mistake in fact or law: Provided further that the existence of any separate oral agreement as to any matter on which a document is silent, and which is not inconsistent with its terms, may be proved. In considering whether or not this proviso applies, the Court shall have regard to the degree of formality of the document: Provided also that the existence of any separate oral agreement, constituting a condition precedent to the attaching of any obligation under any such contract, grant or disposition of property, may be proved: Provided also that the existence of any distinct subsequent oral agreement to rescind or modify any such contract, grant or disposition of property, may be proved, except in Evidence of terms of contracts, grants and other dispositions of property reduced to form of document. Exclusion of evidence of oral agreement.
summary: Section 95 reinforces the exclusion of oral evidence once a document has been proved under Section 94, but allows oral evidence to prove facts that invalidate the document or to establish separate agreements that are not inconsistent with the written terms.
ingredients: No oral evidence to contradict, vary, add to, or subtract from the terms of a proved document.
Oral evidence may be admitted to prove facts that would invalidate the document (e.g., fraud, intimidation, illegality, lack of execution, incapacity, lack of consideration, mistake).
Separate oral agreements on matters silent in the document, and not inconsistent with it, may be proved.
The court must consider the degree of formality of the document when applying the proviso.
Oral agreements that are conditions precedent to the obligation may be proved.
Subsequent oral agreements to rescind or modify the contract may be proved, except where the document itself governs the terms.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 95, Page 29

description: Whoever desires any Court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts must prove that those facts exist, and when a person is bound to prove the existence of any fact, it is said that the burden of proof lies on that person. Illustrations. ( a ) A desires a Court to give judgment that B shall be punished for a crime which A says B has committed. A must prove that B has committed the crime. ( b ) A desires a Court to give judgment that he is entitled to certain land in the possession of B, by reason of facts which he asserts, and which B denies, to be true.Amust prove the existence of those facts.
summary: A party who seeks a judicial decision based on certain facts must prove those facts. The burden of proof rests on the person who asserts the existence of the fact unless a law says otherwise.
ingredients: The party asserting a fact must prove its existence.
The burden of proof lies on the person who wishes the court to accept the fact.
explanation: Illustrations. ( a ) A desires a Court to give judgment that B shall be punished for a crime which A says B has committed. A must prove that B has committed the crime. ( b ) A desires a Court to give judgment that he is entitled to certain land in the possession of B, by reason of facts which he asserts, and which B denies, to be true.Amust prove the existence of those facts.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 104, Page 32

description: The burden of proof in a suit or proceeding lies on that person who would fail if no evidence at all were given on either side. Illustrations. ( a ) A sues B for land of which B is in possession, and which, as A asserts, was left to Aby the will of C, B's father. If no evidence were given on either side, B would be entitled to retain his possession. Therefore, the burden of proof is on A. ( b ) A sues B for money due on a bond. The execution of the bond is admitted, but B says that it was obtained by fraud, whichAdenies. If no evidence were given on either side, A would succeed, as the bond is not disputed and the fraud is not proved. Therefore, the burden of proof is on B.
summary: The party who would be unsuccessful if no evidence were presented bears the burden of proof. This is determined by considering which side would prevail in the absence of any evidence.
ingredients: Identify the party who would fail if no evidence were given.
That party bears the burden of proof in the proceeding.
explanation: Illustrations. ( a ) A sues B for land of which B is in possession, and which, as A asserts, was left to Aby the will of C, B's father. If no evidence were given on either side, B would be entitled to retain his possession. Therefore, the burden of proof is on A. ( b ) A sues B for money due on a bond. The execution of the bond is admitted, but B says that it was obtained by fraud, whichAdenies. If no evidence were given on either side, A would succeed, as the bond is not disputed and the fraud is not proved. Therefore, the burden of proof is on B.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 105, Page 32

description: The burden of proof as to any particular fact lies on that person who wishes the Court to believe in its existence, unless it is provided by any law that the proof of that fact shall lie on any particular person. Illustration. A pr
summary: For each specific fact, the party who wants the court to accept that fact bears the burden of proving it, unless a statute assigns the burden to someone else.
ingredients: The party seeking to prove a particular fact must bear the burden of proof for that fact.
If a law expressly assigns the burden to another person, that assignment prevails.
explanation: Illustration. A pr
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 106, Page 32

description: The burden of proving any fact necessary to be proved in order to enable any person to give evidence of any other fact is on the person who wishes to give such evidence. Illustrations. ( a ) A wishes to prove a dying declaration by B. A must prove B's death. ( b ) A wishes to prove, by secondary evidence, the contents of a lost document. A must prove that the document has been lost.
summary: Section 107 places the onus on the party seeking to introduce evidence on proving any prerequisite fact needed for that evidence. The party must also prove the existence of the evidence itself, such as a dying declaration or a lost document.
ingredients: Burden of proving prerequisite facts rests on the person who wishes to give the evidence
For a dying declaration, the proponent must prove the declarant's death
For secondary evidence of a lost document, the proponent must prove the document is lost
explanation: ( a ) A wishes to prove a dying declaration by B. A must prove B's death. ( b ) A wishes to prove, by secondary evidence, the contents of a lost document. A must prove that the document has been lost.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 107, Page 32

description: When a person is accused of any offence, the burden of proving the existence of circumstances bringing the case within any of the General Exceptions in the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or within any special exception or proviso contained in any other part of the said Sanhita, or in any law defining the offence, is upon him, and the Court shall presume the absence of such circumstances.
summary: Section 108 requires an accused to prove any circumstance that would place the case under a general or special exception. If the accused fails to do so, the court will presume that such circumstances do not exist.
ingredients: Accused must prove existence of circumstances that invoke a General Exception
Accused must prove existence of circumstances that invoke any special exception or proviso
If not proved, the court presumes the absence of such circumstances
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 108, Page 32

description: When any fact is especially within the knowledge of any person, the burden of proving that fact is upon him. Illustrations. ( a ) When a person does an act with some intention other than that which the character and circumstances of the act suggest, the burden of proving that intention is upon him. ( b ) A is charged with travelling on a railway without a ticket. The burden of proving that he had a ticket is on him.
summary: Section 109 places the burden of proving a fact that is within a person's special knowledge on that person. This includes proving subjective intention or the existence of a ticket when charged with travelling without one.
ingredients: If a fact is especially within a person's knowledge, that person must prove it
Proof of intention contrary to outward conduct rests on the person claiming it
Proof of possession of a ticket when charged with travelling without one rests on the accused
explanation: ( a ) When a person does an act with some intention other than that which the character and circumstances of the act suggest, the burden of proving that intention is upon him. ( b ) A is charged with travelling on a railway without a ticket. The burden of proving that he had a ticket is on him.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 109, Page 33

description: When the question is whether a man is alive or dead, and it is shown that he was alive within thirty years, the burden of proving that he is dead is on the person who affirms it.
summary: Section 110 states that if a person was known to be alive within the last thirty years, the party asserting his death must prove that death.
ingredients: If a man was alive within the past thirty years, the burden of proving his death lies on the party claiming death
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 110, Page 33

description: When the question is whether a man is alive or dead, and it is proved that he has not been heard of for seven years by those who would naturally have heard of him if he had been alive, the burden of proving that he is alive is shifted to the person who affirms it.
summary: Section 111 reverses the burden when a person has been unheard of for seven years; the party asserting that he is alive must prove his existence.
ingredients: If a man has not been heard of for seven years by those who would normally know, the burden of proving he is alive shifts to the party asserting his aliveness
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 111, Page 33

description: When the question is whether persons are partners, landlord and tenant, or principal and agent, and it has been shown that they have been acting as such, the burden of proving that they do not stand, or have ceased to stand, to each other in those relationships respectively, is on the person who affirms it.
summary: Section 112 places the burden on the party denying an existing relationship (partnership, tenancy, agency) to prove that the relationship does not exist or has ended.
ingredients: If parties have acted as partners, landlord‑tenant, or principal‑agent, the party denying the relationship must prove its non‑existence or termination
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 112, Page 33

description: When the question is whether any person is owner of anything of which he is shown to be in possession, the burden of proving that he is not the owner is on the person who affirms that he is not the owner.
summary: Section 113 requires the party claiming non‑ownership of property in possession to prove that lack of ownership.
ingredients: If a person is in possession of an item, the party asserting he is not the owner must prove non‑ownership
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 113, Page 33

description: Where there is a question as to the good faith of a transaction between parties, one of whom stands to the other in a position of active confidence, the burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the party who is in a position of active confidence. Illustrations. ( a ) The good faith of a sale by a client to an advocate is in question in a suit brought by the client. The burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the advocate. ( b ) The good faith of a sale by a son just come of age to a father is in question in a suit brought by the son. The burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the father.
summary: Section 114 places the burden of proving good faith in a transaction on the party who enjoys a position of active confidence, such as a client‑advocate or a father‑son relationship.
ingredients: If a transaction's good faith is questioned, the party in a position of active confidence must prove good faith
explanation: ( a ) The good faith of a sale by a client to an advocate is in question in a suit brought by the client. The burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the advocate. ( b ) The good faith of a sale by a son just come of age to a father is in question in a suit brought by the son. The burden of proving the good faith of the transaction is on the father.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 114, Page 33

description: ( 1 ) Where a person is accused of having committed any offence specified in sub-section ( 2 ), in— ( a ) any area declared to be a disturbed area under any enactment for the time being in force, making provision for the suppression of disorder and restoration and maintenance of public order; or ( b ) any area in which there has been, over a period of more than one month, extensive disturbance of the public peace, and it is shown that such person had been at a place in such area at a time when firearms or explosives were used at or from that place to attack or resist the members of any armed forces or the forces charged with the maintenance of public order acting in the discharge of their duties, it shall be presumed, unless the contrary is shown, that such person had committed such offence. ( 2 ) The offences referred to in sub-section ( 1 ) are the following, namely:— ( a ) an offence under section 147, section 148, section 149 or section 150 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; ( b ) criminal conspiracy or attempt to commit, or abetment of, an offence under section 149 or section 150 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
summary: Section 115 creates a presumption of guilt for persons present in disturbed areas during violent incidents, unless they can prove otherwise. It lists specific offences to which the presumption applies.
ingredients: If a person is accused of offences listed in sub‑section (2) and was present in a declared disturbed area or an area with prolonged public disorder, a presumption of commission of the offence arises
The presumption applies unless the accused can show contrary evidence
Offences covered include sections 147, 148, 149, 150 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita and related conspiracy or abetment
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 115, Page 34

description: The fact that any person was born during the continuance of a valid marriage between his mother and any man, or within two hundred and eighty days after its dissolution, the mother remaining unmarried, shall be conclusive proof that he is the legitimate child of that man, unless it can be shown that the parties to the marriage had no access to each other at any time when he could have been begotten.
summary: Section 116 provides that a child born within a marriage or within 280 days after its dissolution is presumed legitimate, unless the parties had no opportunity for sexual relations during the relevant period.
ingredients: Birth during a valid marriage or within 280 days after its dissolution creates a conclusive presumption of legitimacy
The presumption can be rebutted by showing that the spouses had no access to each other during the conception period
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 116, Page 34

description: When the question is whether the commission of suicide by a woman had been abetted by her husband or any relative of her husband and it is shown that she had committed suicide within a period of seven years from the date of her marriage and that her husband or such relative of her husband had subjected her to cruelty, the Court may presume, having regard to all the other circumstances of the case, that such suicide had been abetted by her husband or by such relative of her husband. Explanation. —For th
summary: Section 117 allows the court to presume abetment of a woman's suicide by her husband or his relative when the suicide occurs within seven years of marriage and cruelty is established, subject to consideration of all circumstances.
ingredients: If a woman commits suicide within seven years of marriage and cruelty by husband or his relative is shown, the court may presume abetment
explanation: Explanation. —For th
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 117, Page 33

description: e purposes of this section, "cruelty" shall have the same meaning as in section 86 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. When the question is whether a person has committed the dowry death of a woman and it is shown that soon before her death, such woman had been subjected by such person to cruelty or harassment for, or in connection with, any demand for dowry, the Court shall presume that such person had caused the dowry death. Explanation. —For the purposes of this section, "dowry death" shall have the same meaning as in section 80 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
summary: If a woman dies and it is shown she was subjected to cruelty or harassment related to dowry shortly before death, the court presumes the accused caused the dowry death. The terms "cruelty" and "dowry death" are defined by reference to sections 86 and 80 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
ingredients: Court shall presume the accused caused dowry death if cruelty or harassment for dowry is shown before death
"Cruelty" means as defined in section 86 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
"Dowry death" means as defined in section 80 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
explanation: Explanation. —For the purposes of this section, "dowry death" shall have the same meaning as in section 80 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 118, Page 34

description: (1) The Court may presume the existence of any fact which it thinks likely to have happened, regard being had to the common course of natural events, human conduct and public and private business, in their relation to the facts of the particular case. Illustrations. The Court may presume that— (a) a man who is in possession of stolen goods soon, after the theft is either the thief or has received the goods knowing them to be stolen, unless he can account for his possession; (b) an accomplice is unworthy of credit, unless he is corroborated in material particulars; Presumption as to certain offences. Birth during marriage, conclusive proof of legitimacy. Presumption as to abetment of suicide by a married woman. Presumption as to dowry death. Court may presume existence of certain facts. (2) The Court shall also have regard to such facts as the following, in considering whether such maxims do or do not apply to the particular case before it:— (i) as to Illustration (a)—a shop-keeper has in his bill a marked rupee soon after it was stolen, and cannot account for its possession specifically, but is continually receiving rupees in the course of his business; (ii) as to Illustration (b)—A, a person of the highest character, is tried for causing a man's death by an act of negligence in arranging certain machinery. B, a person of equally good character, who also took part in the arrangement, describes precisely what was done, and admits and explains the common carelessness of A and himself; (iii) as to Illustration (b) — a crime is committed by several persons. A, B and C, three of the criminals, are captured on the spot and kept apart from each other. Each gives an account of the crime implicating D, and the accounts corroborate each other in such a manner as to render previous concert highly improbable; (iv) as to Illustration (c) — A, the drawer of a bill of exchange, was a man of business. B, the acceptor, was a young and ignorant person, completely under A's influence; (v) as to Illustration (d) — it is proved that a river ran in a certain course five years ago, but it is known that there have been floods since that time which might change its course; (vi) as to Illustration (e) — a judicial act, the regularity of which is in question, was performed under exceptional circumstances; (vii) as to Illustration (f) — the question is, whether a letter was received. It is shown to have been posted, but the usual course of the post was interrupted by disturbances; (viii) as to Illustration (g) — a man refuses to produce a document which would bear on a contract of small importance on which he is sued, but which might also injure the feelings and reputation of his family; (ix) as to Illustration (h) — a man refuses to answer a question which is not compelled by law, but the answer might cause loss to him in matters unconnected with the matter in relation to which it is asked; (x) as to Illustration (i) — a bond is in possession of the obligor, but the circumstances of the case are such that he may have stolen it.
summary: The court may presume facts it deems likely based on natural course of events and business practices, with numerous illustrations provided. It also considers specific factual circumstances to decide whether presumptions apply. The section lists illustrative scenarios to guide the court's discretion.
ingredients: Court may presume existence of any fact it thinks likely, considering natural events and business practices
Illustrations provide examples of presumptions (stolen goods, credibility of accomplice, etc.)
Court must consider specific factual illustrations before applying presumptions
explanation: Illustrations. The Court may presume that— (a) a man who is in possession of stolen goods soon, after the theft is either the thief or has received the goods knowing them to be stolen, unless he can account for his possession; (b) an accomplice is unworthy of credit, unless he is corroborated in material particulars; ... (full illustrations as listed in the description)
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 119, Page 34

description: In a prosecution for rape under sub-section (2) of section 64 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, where sexual intercourse by the accused is proved and the question is whether it was without the consent of the woman alleged to have been raped and such woman states in her evidence before the Court that she did not consent, the Court shall presume that she did not consent. Presumption as to absence of consent in certain prosecution for rape. Sec. 1] T HE GAZETTE OF INDIA EXTRAORDINAR Y 3 5 Explanation. —In this section, "sexual intercourse" shall mean any of the acts mentioned in section 63 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
summary: When a woman testifies that she did not consent to sexual intercourse that has been proved, the court must presume lack of consent in rape prosecutions under section 64(2). The term "sexual intercourse" is defined by reference to section 63 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
ingredients: If sexual intercourse is proved and the woman states she did not consent, the court presumes lack of consent
"Sexual intercourse" is defined by reference to section 63 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
explanation: Explanation. —In this section, "sexual intercourse" shall mean any of the acts mentioned in section 63 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 120, Page 35

description: When one person has, by his declaration, act or omission, intentionally caused or permitted another person to believe a thing to be true and to act upon such belief, neither he nor his representative shall be allowed, in any suit or proceeding between himself and such person or his representative, to deny the truth of that thing. Illustration. A intentionally and falsely leads B to believe that certain land belongs to A, and thereby induces B to buy and pay for it. The land afterwards becomes the property of A, and A seeks to set aside the sale on the ground that, at the time of the sale, he had no title. He must not be allowed to prove his want of title.
summary: A party who induces another to believe a false fact and act on it cannot later deny that fact in any legal proceeding. The illustration shows a seller cannot deny title after inducing the buyer to purchase based on a false representation.
ingredients: If a person intentionally causes another to believe a false fact, he cannot deny that fact later in any suit
Applies to both the person and his representative
explanation: Illustration. A intentionally and falsely leads B to believe that certain land belongs to A, and thereby induces B to buy and pay for it. The land afterwards becomes the property of A, and A seeks to set aside the sale on the ground that, at the time of the sale, he had no title. He must not be allowed to prove his want of title.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 121, Page 36

description: No tenant of immovable property, or person claiming through such tenant, shall, during the continuance of the tenancy or any time thereafter, be permitted to deny that the landlord of such tenant had, at the beginning of the tenancy, a title to such immovable property; and no person who came upon any immovable property by the licence of the person in possession thereof shall be permitted to deny that such person had a title to such possession at the time when such licence was given.
summary: A tenant or anyone claiming through a tenant cannot deny the landlord's title at the start of the tenancy. Similarly, a person who obtained property by licence cannot deny the licensor's title at the time of the licence.
ingredients: Tenant cannot deny landlord's title at the beginning of tenancy
Person who obtained property by licence cannot deny licensor's title at the time of licence
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 122, Page 36

description: No acceptor of a bill of exchange shall be permitted to deny that the drawer had authority to draw such bill or to endorse it; nor shall any bailee or licensee
summary: An acceptor of a bill of exchange cannot deny that the drawer had authority to issue or endorse the bill; the provision also restricts bailees or licensees, though the text is incomplete.
ingredients: Acceptor cannot deny drawer's authority to draw or endorse the bill
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 123, Page 36

description: All persons shall be competent to testify unless the Court considers that they are prevented from understanding the questions put to them, or from giving rational answers to those questions, by tender years, extreme old age, disease, whether of body or mind, or any other cause of the same kind.
summary: Any person may give testimony unless the court finds that age, illness, or similar factors prevent them from understanding the questions or giving rational answers.
ingredients: All persons are competent witnesses
Competence is excluded if the court determines inability to understand or answer due to age, illness, or similar causes
explanation: A person of unsound mind is not incompetent to testify, unless he is prevented by his unsoundness of mind from understanding the questions put to him and giving rational answers to them.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 124, Page 36

description: A witness who is unable to speak may give his evidence in any other manner in which he can make it intelligible, as by writing or by signs; but such writing must be written and the signs made in open Court and evidence so given shall be deemed to be oral evidence: Provided that if the witness is unable to communicate verbally, the Court shall take the assistance of an interpreter or a special educator in recording the statement, and such statement shall be videographed.
summary: A mute witness may testify by writing or signs in open court, which will be treated as oral evidence; the court must provide an interpreter or special educator and videograph the statement.
ingredients: Witness unable to speak may give evidence by writing or signs
Writing and signs must be made in open Court
Such evidence is deemed oral evidence
Court must provide interpreter or special educator if verbal communication is impossible
The statement must be videographed
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 125, Page 36

description: (1) In all civil proceedings the parties to the suit, and the husband or wife of any party to the suit, shall be competent witnesses. (2) In criminal proceedings against any person, the husband or wife of such person, respectively, shall be a competent witness.
summary: Both parties and their spouses are competent witnesses in civil cases, and a spouse is a competent witness in criminal cases against the other spouse.
ingredients: Parties to a civil suit are competent witnesses
Spouses of parties in a civil suit are competent witnesses
Spouse of an accused is a competent witness in criminal proceedings against that accused
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 126, Page 37

description: No Judge or Magistrate shall, except upon the special order of some Court to which he is subordinate, be compelled to answer any question as to his own conduct in Court as such Judge or Magistrate, or as to anything which came to his knowledge in Court as such Judge or Magistrate; but he may be examined as to other matters which occurred in his presence whilst he was so acting.
summary: Judges and magistrates cannot be forced to answer questions about their own conduct or knowledge in court unless a higher court orders it, but they can be examined on other matters they witnessed.
ingredients: Judges/Magistrates are not compelled to answer questions about their own conduct or knowledge
Compulsion is possible only by special order of a superior court
They may be examined on other matters that occurred in their presence
explanation: Illustrations. (a) A, on his trial before the Court of Session, says that a deposition was improperly taken by B, the Magistrate. B cannot be compelled to answer questions as to this, except upon the special order of a superior Court. (b) A is accused before the Court of Session of having given false evidence before B, a Magistrate. B cannot be asked what A said, except upon the special order of the superior Court. (c) A is accused before the Court of Session of attempting to murder a police officer whilst on his trial before B, a Sessions Judge. B may be examined as to what occurred.
