sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 127, Page 37

description: No person who is or has been married, shall be compelled to disclose any communication made to him during marriage by any person to whom he is or has been married; nor shall he be permitted to disclose any such communication, unless the person who made it, or his representative in interest, consents, except in suits between married persons, or proceedings in which one married person is prosecuted for any crime committed against the other.
summary: Married individuals are protected from being forced to disclose marital communications unless they consent, except in disputes between spouses or criminal cases where one spouse is prosecuted for an offense against the other.
ingredients: Married or formerly married persons cannot be compelled to disclose communications made during marriage
Disclosure is allowed only with consent of the maker or their representative
Exceptions: suits between married persons and criminal prosecutions where one spouse is charged for a crime against the other
exceptions: except in suits between married persons, or proceedings in which one married person is prosecuted for any crime committed against the other
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 128, Page 37

description: No one shall be permitted to give any evidence derived from unpublished official records relating to any affairs of State, except with the permission of the officer at the head of the department concerned, who shall give or withhold such permission as he thinks fit.
summary: Evidence from unpublished official state records is prohibited unless the head of the concerned department grants permission.
ingredients: Unpublished official records relating to state affairs cannot be used as evidence
Permission must be obtained from the head officer of the concerned department
The officer may grant or withhold permission at his discretion
exceptions: except with the permission of the officer at the head of the department concerned, who shall give or withhold such permission as he thinks fit
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 129, Page 37

description: No public officer shall be compelled to disclose communications made to him in official confidence, when he considers that the public interests would suffer by the disclosure.
summary: Public officers are protected from being forced to reveal confidential official communications if they believe disclosure would harm public interest.
ingredients: Public officers cannot be compelled to disclose confidential official communications
Discretion is based on the officer's consideration of public interest
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 130, Page 37

description: No Magistrate or police officer shall be compelled to say when he got any information as to the commission of any offence, and no revenue officer shall be compelled to say when he got any information as to the commission of an offence against the public revenue.
summary: Magistrates, police officers, and revenue officers cannot be forced to disclose the time they received information about an offence.
ingredients: Magistrates and police officers are not compelled to state when they received offence-related information
Revenue officers are similarly protected regarding information about offences against public revenue
explanation: Explanation. —"revenue officer" means any officer employed in or about the business of any branch of the public revenue.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 131, Page 37

description: (1) No advocate, shall at any time be permitted, unless with his client's express consent, to disclose any communication made to him in the course and for the purpose of his service as such advocate, by or on behalf of his client, or to state the contents or condition of any document with which he has become acquainted in the course and for the purpose of his professional service, or to disclose any advice given by him to his client in the course and for the purpose of such service: Provided that nothing in this section shall protect from disclosure of— (a) any such communication made in furtherance of any illegal purpose; (b) any fact observed by any advocate, in the course of his service as such, showing that any crime or fraud has been committed since the commencement of his service. (2) It is immaterial whether the attention of such advocate referred to in the proviso to sub-section (1), was or was not directed to such fact by or on behalf of his client.
summary: Advocates may not disclose client communications or documents without client consent, except for communications made for illegal purposes or observed crimes, and the protection continues after the professional relationship ends.
ingredients: Advocates cannot disclose client communications or documents without express client consent
Protection does not extend to communications made in furtherance of illegal purpose
Protection does not extend to facts observed by the advocate indicating crime or fraud after commencement of service
The obligation continues after the professional relationship ends
The attention of the advocate to a fact is immaterial to the protection
exceptions: nothing in this section shall protect from disclosure of— (a) any such communication made in furtherance of any illegal purpose; (b) any fact observed by any advocate, in the course of his service as such, showing that any crime or fraud has been committed since the commencement of his service
explanation: Explanation. —The obligation stated in this section continues after the professional service has ceased. Illustrations. (a) A, a client, says to B, an advocate—"I have committed forgery, and I wish you to defend me". As the defence of a man known to be guilty is not a criminal purpose, this communication is protected from disclosure. (b) A, a client, says to B, an advocate—"I wish to obtain possession of property by the use of a forged deed on which I request you to sue". This communication, being made in furtherance of a criminal purpose, is not protected from disclosure. (c) A, being charged with embezzlement, retains B, an advocate, to defend him. In the course of the proceedings, B observes that an entry has been made in A's account book, charging A with the sum said to have been embezzled, which entry was not in the book at the commencement of his professional service. This being a fact observed by B in the course of his service, showing that a fraud has been committed since the commencement of the proceedings, it is
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 132, Page 37

description: If any party to a suit gives evidence therein at his own instance or otherwise, he shall not be deemed to have consented thereby to such disclosure as is mentioned in section 132; and, if any party to a suit or proceeding calls any such advocate, as a witness, he shall be deemed to have consented to such disclosure only if he questions such advocate, on matters which, but for such question, he would not be at liberty to disclose.
summary: A party who gives evidence is not considered to have consented to disclosure of privileged communications, except when he questions his own advocate on matters he otherwise could not disclose.
ingredients: Party giving evidence is not deemed to have consented to disclosure of privileged communication.
Consent is deemed only if the party questions his own advocate on matters he would otherwise not be at liberty to disclose.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 133, Page 38

description: No one shall be compelled to disclose to the Court any confidential communication which has taken place between him and his legal adviser, unless he offers himself as a witness, in which case he may be compelled to disclose any such communications as may appear to the Court necessary to be known in order to explain any evidence which he has given, but no others.
summary: A person cannot be forced to reveal confidential communications with a legal adviser unless he becomes a witness, and then only the communications necessary to explain his evidence may be disclosed.
ingredients: No compulsion to disclose confidential communication with legal adviser.
If the person becomes a witness, disclosure is limited to communications necessary to explain his evidence.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 134, Page 38

description: No witness who is not a party to a suit shall be compelled to produce his title-deeds to any property, or any document in virtue of which he holds any property as pledgee or mortgagee or any document the production of which might tend to criminate him, unless he has agreed in writing to produce them with the person seeking the production of such deeds or some person through whom he claims.
summary: A non‑party witness cannot be forced to produce title deeds or related documents that could incriminate him unless he has given a written agreement to do so.
ingredients: Non‑party witness not compelled to produce title‑deeds or documents that may incriminate him.
Production is required only if the witness has given a written agreement to produce the documents.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 135, Page 38

description: No one shall be compelled to produce documents in his possession or electronic records under his control, which any other person would be entitled to refuse to produce if they were in his possession or control, unless such last-mentioned person consents to their production.
summary: A person cannot be forced to produce documents or electronic records in his possession unless the person who would otherwise be entitled to refuse consents to their production.
ingredients: No compulsion to produce documents or electronic records in possession.
Production is permissible only with consent of the person who could otherwise refuse to produce them.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 136, Page 38

description: A witness shall not be excused from answering any question as to any matter relevant to the matter in issue in any suit or in any civil or criminal proceeding, upon the ground that the answer to such question will criminate, or may tend directly or indirectly to criminate, such witness, or that it will expose, or tend directly or indirectly to expose, such witness to a penalty or forfeiture of any kind: Provided that no such answer, which a witness shall be compelled to give, shall subject him to any arrest or prosecution, or be proved against him in any criminal proceeding, except a prosecution forgiving false evidence by such answer.
summary: A witness cannot refuse to answer relevant questions on self‑incrimination grounds, but the answer cannot be used to arrest, prosecute, or convict the witness, except for prosecution for giving false evidence.
ingredients: Witness cannot be excused from answering relevant questions on self‑incrimination grounds.
Answer given cannot lead to arrest or prosecution, nor be used against the witness in criminal proceedings, except for false evidence prosecution.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 137, Page 38

description: An accomplice shall be a competent witness against an accused person; and a conviction is not illegal if it proceeds upon the corroborated testimony of an accomplice.
summary: An accomplice is deemed a competent witness, and a conviction based on corroborated accomplice testimony is lawful.
ingredients: Accomplice is a competent witness against the accused.
Conviction based on corroborated accomplice testimony is not illegal.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 138, Page 39

description: No particular number of witnesses shall in any case be required for the proof of any fact.
summary: The law does not prescribe a fixed number of witnesses required to prove any fact.
ingredients: No mandatory number of witnesses for proof of a fact.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 139, Page 39

description: The order in which witnesses are produced and examined shall be regulated by the law and practice for the time being relating to civil and criminal procedure respectively, and, in the absence of any such law, by the discretion of the Court.
summary: Witness production and examination follow existing procedural law; if none exists, the court may decide the order.
ingredients: Order of witness production and examination governed by existing civil and criminal procedural law.
If no such law exists, the court has discretion to determine the order.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 140, Page 39

description: ( 1 ) When either party proposes to give evidence of any fact, the Judge may ask the party proposing to give the evidence in what manner the alleged fact, if proved, would be relevant; and the Judge shall admit the evidence if he thinks that the fact, if proved, would be relevant, and not otherwise. ( 2 ) If the fact proposed to be proved is one of which evidence is admissible only upon proof of some other fact, such last mentioned fact must be proved before evidence is given of the fact first mentioned, unless the party undertakes to give proof of such fact, and the Court is satisfied with such undertaking. ( 3 ) If the relevancy of one alleged fact depends upon another alleged fact being first proved, the Judge may, in his discretion, either permit evidence of the first fact to be given before the second fact is proved, or require evidence of the second fact before evidence is given of the first fact.
explanation: Illustrations. ( a ) It is proposed to prove a statement about a relevant fact by a person alleged to be dead, which statement is relevant under section 26. The fact that the person is dead must be proved by the person proposing to prove the statement, before evidence is given of the statement. ( b ) It is proposed to prove, by a copy, the contents of a document said to be lost. The fact that the original is lost must be proved by the person proposing to produce the copy, before the copy is produced. ( c ) A is accused of receiving stolen property knowing it to have been stolen. It is proposed to prove that he denied the possession of the property. The relevancy of the denial depends on the identity of the property. The Court may, in its discretion, either require the property to be identified before the denial of the possession is proved, or permit the denial of the possession to be proved before the property is identified. ( d ) It is proposed to prove a fact A which is said to have been the cause or effect of a fact in issue. There are several intermediate facts B, C and D which must be shown to exist before the fact A can be regarded as the cause or effect of the fact in issue. The Court may either permit Ato be proved before B, C or D is proved, or may require proof of B, C and D before permitting proof of A.
summary: The judge decides admissibility based on relevance, may require prior proof of prerequisite facts, and has discretion on the order of proving inter‑dependent facts; illustrations clarify the application.
ingredients: Judge may inquire into relevance of proposed evidence before admitting it.
If evidence of a fact depends on another fact, the prerequisite fact must be proved first unless an undertaking is given.
Judge may, at his discretion, order the sequence of proof when relevance of facts is inter‑dependent.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 141, Page 39

description: ( 1 ) The examination of a witness by the party who calls him shall be called his examination-in-chief. ( 2 ) The examination of a witness by the adverse party shall be called his cross-examination. ( 3 ) The examination of a witness, subsequent to the cross-examination, by the party who called him, shall be called his re-examination.
summary: Defines examination-in-chief, cross‑examination, and re‑examination of a witness.
ingredients: Examination-in-chief: examination by the party who called the witness.
Cross‑examination: examination by the adverse party.
Re‑examination: subsequent examination by the party who called the witness.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 142, Page 39

description: ( 1 ) Witnesses shall be first examined-in-chief, then (if the adverse party so desires) cross-examined, then (if the party calling him so desires) re-examined. ( 2 ) The examination-in-chief and cross-examination must relate to relevant facts, but the cross-examination need not be confined to the facts to which the witness testified on his examination-in-chief. ( 3 ) The re-examination shall be directed to the explanation of matters referred to in cross-examination; and, if new matter is, by permission of the Court, introduced in re-examination
summary: Specifies the sequence of examination‑in‑chief, cross‑examination and re‑examination, and outlines the scope of each stage.
ingredients: Order of examination: chief, then cross (if desired), then re‑examination (if desired).
Examination‑in‑chief and cross‑examination must relate to relevant facts; cross‑examination may go beyond chief testimony.
Re‑examination is for explaining cross‑examination matters; new matter may be introduced with court permission.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 143, Page 40

description: A person summoned to produce a document does not become a witness by the mere fact that he produces it, and cannot be cross-examined unless and until he is called as a witness.
summary: A person who is called only to produce a document is not deemed a witness and cannot be cross‑examined until formally summoned as a witness.
ingredients: Summoned to produce a document does not make one a witness
Cross‑examination only after being called as a witness
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 144, Page 40

description: Witnesses to character may be cross-examined and re-examined.
summary: A witness whose testimony concerns a person's character can be cross‑examined and re‑examined.
ingredients: Cross‑examination of character witnesses permitted
Re‑examination of character witnesses permitted
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 145, Page 40

description: ( 1 ) Any question suggesting the answer which the person putting it wishes or expects to receive, is called a leading question. ( 2 ) Leading questions must not, if objected to by the adverse party, be asked in an examination-in-chief, or in a re-examination, except with the permission of the Court. ( 3 ) The Court shall permit leading questions as to matters which are introductory or undisputed, or which have, in its opinion, been already sufficiently proved. ( 4 ) Leading questions may be asked in cross-examination.
summary: A leading question suggests its own answer. Such questions cannot be asked in examination‑in‑chief or re‑examination without the Court’s permission, but may be allowed for introductory, undisputed matters or in cross‑examination.
ingredients: Definition of leading question
Leading questions not allowed in examination‑in‑chief or re‑examination without Court’s permission
Court may permit leading questions for introductory or undisputed matters
Leading questions allowed in cross‑examination
exceptions: except with the permission of the Court
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 146, Page 40

description: Any witness may be asked, while under examination, whether any contract, grant or other disposition of property, as to which he is giving evidence, was not contained in a document, and if he says that it was, or if he is about to make any statement as to the contents of any document, which, in the opinion of the Court, ought to be produced, the adverse party may object to such evidence being given until such document is produced, or until facts have been proved which entitle the party who called the witness to give secondary evidence of it. Explanation. —A witness may give oral evidence of statements made by other persons about the contents of documents if such statements are in themselves relevant facts. Illustration. The question is, whether A assaulted B. C deposes that he heard A say to D—"B wrote a letter accusing me of theft, and I will be revenged on him". This statement is relevant, as showing A's motive for the assault, and evidence may be given of it, though no other evidence is given about the letter.
summary: A witness can be questioned about the existence or contents of a document, but the Court may require the document to be produced or secondary evidence before such testimony is admitted. Oral evidence of others’ statements about a document’s contents is admissible if relevant.
ingredients: Witness may be asked about existence of a document
Adverse party may object until document is produced or secondary evidence proved
Oral evidence of others’ statements about document contents admissible if relevant
explanation: A witness may give oral evidence of statements made by other persons about the contents of documents if such statements are in themselves relevant facts. Illustration: The question is, whether A assaulted B. C deposes that he heard A say to D—"B wrote a letter accusing me of theft, and I will be revenged on him". This statement is relevant, as showing A's motive for the assault, and evidence may be given of it, though no other evidence is given about the letter.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 147, Page 40

description: A witness may be cross-examined as to previous statements made by him in writing or reduced into writing, and relevant to matters in question, without such writing being shown to him, or being proved; but, if it is intended to contradict him by the writing, his attention must, before the writing can be proved, be called to those parts of it which are to be used for the purpose of contradicting him.
summary: A witness can be cross‑examined about his prior written statements without producing the document, but if the purpose is to contradict him, the relevant portions must be pointed out before the writing is proved.
ingredients: Cross‑examination about prior written statements permitted
Writing need not be shown or proved
If intended to contradict, relevant parts must be highlighted before proof
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 148, Page 40

description: When a witness is cross-examined, he may, in addition to the questions hereinbefore referred to, be asked any questions which tend— ( a ) to test his veracity; or ( b ) to discover who he is and what is his position in life; or ( c ) to shake his credit, by injuring his character, although the answer to such questions might tend directly or indirectly to criminate him, or might expose or tend directly or indirectly to expose him to a penalty or forfeiture: Order of examinations. Cross- examination of person called to produce a document. Witnesses to character. Leading questions. Evidence as to matters in writing. Cross- examination as to previous statements in writing. Questions lawful in cross- examination.
summary: During cross‑examination, a witness may be asked questions to test truthfulness, ascertain personal background, or attack credibility, even if the answers could indirectly incriminate or expose the witness to penalty.
ingredients: Questions to test veracity
Questions to discover personal background
Questions to attack credit/character
Answers may indirectly criminate or expose to penalty
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 149, Page 40

description: If any such question relates to a matter relevant to the suit or proceeding, the provisions of section 137 shall apply thereto.
summary: When a character‑related question is relevant to the case, the rules of Section 137 govern its admissibility.
ingredients: Relevant character question → apply Section 137
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 150, Page 41

description: ( 1 ) If any such question relates to a matter not relevant to the suit or proceeding, except in so far as it affects the credit of the witness by injuring his character, the Court shall decide whether or not the witness shall be compelled to answer it, and may, if it thinks fit, warn the witness that he is not obliged to answer it. ( 2 ) In exercising its discretion, the Court shall have regard to the following considerations, namely:— ( a ) such questions are proper if they are of such a nature that the truth of the imputation conveyed by them would seriously affect the opinion of the Court as to the credibility of the witness on the matter to which he testifies; ( b ) such questions are improper if the imputation which they convey relates to matters so remote in time, or of such a character, that the truth of the imputation would not affect, or would affect in a slight degree, the opinion of the Court as to the credibility of the witness on the matter to which he testifies; ( c ) such questions are improper if there is a great disproportion between the importance of the imputation made against the witness's character and the importance of his evidence; ( d ) the Court may, if it sees fit, draw, from the witness's refusal to answer, the inference that the answer if given would be unfavourable.
summary: For character questions not relevant to the case, the Court decides whether the witness must answer, considering relevance to credibility, remoteness, disproportion, and possible adverse inference.
ingredients: Court decides compulsion for irrelevant character questions
Consideration of impact on credibility
Remote or insignificant imputations are improper
Disproportionate imputations are improper
Possible adverse inference from refusal
exceptions: except in so far as it affects the credit of the witness by injuring his character
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 151, Page 41

description: No such question as is referred to in section 151 ought to be asked, unless the person asking it has reasonable grounds for thinking that the imputation which it conveys is well-founded. Illustrations. ( a ) An advocate is instructed by another advocate that an important witness is a dacoit. This is a reasonable ground for asking the witness whether he is a dacoit. ( b )An advocate is informed by a person in Court that an important witness is a dacoit. The informant, on being questioned by the advocate, gives satisfactory reasons for his statement. This is a reasonable ground for asking the witness whether he is a dacoit. ( c )A witness, of whom nothing whatever is known, is asked at random whether he is a dacoit. There are here no reasonable grounds for the question. ( d ) A witness, of whom nothing whatever is known, being questioned as to his mode of life and means of living, gives unsatisfactory answers. This may be a reasonable ground for asking him if he is a dacoit.
summary: A character question may be asked only if the asker has reasonable grounds to believe the imputation is well‑founded; the section provides illustrations of reasonable and unreasonable grounds.
ingredients: Reasonable grounds required to ask character question
Illustrations of reasonable and unreasonable grounds
explanation: Illustrations. ( a ) An advocate is instructed by another advocate that an important witness is a dacoit. This is a reasonable ground for asking the witness whether he is a dacoit. ( b )An advocate is informed by a person in Court that an important witness is a dacoit. The informant, on being questioned by the advocate, gives satisfactory reasons for his statement. This is a reasonable ground for asking the witness whether he is a dacoit. ( c )A witness, of whom nothing whatever is known, is asked at random whether he is a dacoit. There are here no reasonable grounds for the question. ( d ) A witness, of whom nothing whatever is known, being questioned as to his mode of life and means of living, gives unsatisfactory answers. This may be a reasonable ground for asking him if he is a dacoit.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 152, Page 41

description: If the Court is of opinion that any such question was asked without reasonable grounds, it may, if it was asked by any advocate, report the circumstances of the case to the High Court or other authority to which such advocate is subject in the exercise of his profession. When witness to be compelled to answer. Court to decide when question shall be asked and when witness compelled to answer. Question not to be asked without reasonable grounds. Procedure of Court in case of question being asked without reasonable grounds.
summary: If a character question is asked without reasonable grounds, the Court may report the advocate to the appropriate authority and decide whether the witness must answer.
ingredients: Court may report advocate for improper question
Court decides compulsion to answer
Question not to be asked without reasonable grounds
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 153, Page 41

description: The Court may forbid any questions or inquiries which it regards as indecent or scandalous, although such questions or inquiries may have some bearing on the questions before the Court, unless they relate to facts in issue, or to matters necessary to be known in order to determine whether or not the facts in issue existed.
summary: The court can prohibit questions that are indecent or scandalous, even if they are somewhat relevant, unless they directly relate to the facts in issue or are necessary to determine those facts.
ingredients: Court may forbid indecent or scandalous questions or inquiries
Prohibition applies even if the question has some bearing on the case
Exception when the question relates to facts in issue or matters necessary to determine those facts
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 154, Page 42

description: The Court shall forbid any question which appears to it to be intended to insult or annoy, or which, though proper in itself, appears to the Court needlessly offensive in form.
summary: The court must prohibit any question that seems intended to insult or annoy, or that is unnecessarily offensive in form, even if the question is otherwise proper.
ingredients: Court must forbid questions intended to insult or annoy
Court must forbid questions that are needlessly offensive in form
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 155, Page 42

description: When a witness has been asked and has answered any question which is relevant to the inquiry only in so far as it tends to shake his credit by injuring his character, no evidence shall be given to contradict him; but, if he answers falsely, he may afterwards be charged with giving false evidence.
summary: If a witness is questioned only to attack his credibility, the opposite party cannot introduce evidence to contradict his answer, though a false answer may lead to a charge of giving false evidence.
ingredients: No evidence may be given to contradict a witness when the question only attacks his credit
If the witness answers falsely, he may be charged with giving false evidence
exceptions: If a witness is asked whether he has been previously convicted of any crime and denies it, evidence may be given of his previous conviction.
If a witness is asked any question tending to impeach his impartiality, and answers it by denying the facts suggested, he may be contradicted.
explanation: Illustrations. ( a ) A claim against an underwriter is resisted on the ground of fraud. The claimant is asked whether, in a former transaction, he had not made a fraudulent claim. He denies it. Evidence is offered to show that he did make such a claim. The evidence is inadmissible. ( b ) A witness is asked whether he was not dismissed from a situation for dishonesty. He denies it. Evidence is offered to show that he was dismissed for dishonesty. The evidence is not admissible. ( c )Aaffirms that on a certain day he saw B at Goa.Ais asked whether he himself was not on that day at Varanasi. He denies it. Evidence is offered to show thatAwas on that day at Varanasi. The evidence is admissible, not as contradicting A on a fact which affects his credit, but as contradicting the alleged fact that B was seen on the day in question in Goa. In each of these cases, the witness might, if his denial was false, be charged with giving false evidence. ( d )Ais asked whether his family has not had a blood feud with the family of B against whom he gives evidence. He denies it. He may be contradicted on the ground that the question tends to impeach his impartiality.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 156, Page 42

description: ( 1 ) The Court may, in its discretion, permit the person who calls a witness to put any question to him which might be put in cross-examination by the adverse party. ( 2 ) Nothing in this section shall disentitle the person so permitted under sub-section ( 1 ), to rely on any part of the evidence of such witness.
summary: The court may allow the party calling a witness to ask any question that the opposing party could ask in cross‑examination, and this permission does not prevent reliance on any part of the witness’s evidence.
ingredients: Court may, at its discretion, permit the calling party to ask any question that could be asked in cross‑examination
Permission does not deprive the party of reliance on any part of the witness’s evidence
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 157, Page 42

description: The credit of a witness may be impeached in the following ways by the adverse party, or, with the consent of the Court, by the party who calls him— ( a ) by the evidence of persons who testify that they, from their knowledge of the witness, believe him to be unworthy of credit; ( b ) by proof that the witness has been bribed, or has accepted the offer of a bribe, or has received any other corrupt inducement to give his evidence; ( c ) by proof of former statements inconsistent with any part of his evidence which is liable to be contradicted.
summary: A witness’s credibility can be attacked by showing that others consider him unworthy of credit, that he has taken a bribe or other corrupt inducement, or that his prior statements contradict his current testimony.
ingredients: Impeachment by evidence that persons consider the witness unworthy of credit
Impeachment by proof of bribery or corrupt inducement
Impeachment by proof of former statements inconsistent with current evidence
explanation: Explanation. —A witness declaring another witness to be unworthy of credit may not, upon his examination-in-chief, give reasons for his belief, but he may be asked his reasons in cross-examination, and the answers which he gives cannot be contradicted, though, if they are false, he may afterwards be charged with giving false evidence. Indecent and scandalous questions. Questions intended to insult or annoy. Exclusion of evidence to contradict answers to questions testing veracity. Question by party to his own witness. Impeaching credit of witness.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 158, Page 42

description: When a witness whom it is intended to corroborate gives evidence of any relevant fact, he may be questioned as to any other circumstances which he observed at or near to the time or place at which such relevant fact occurred, if the Court is of opinion that such circumstances, if proved, would corroborate the testimony of the witness as to the relevant fact which he testifies. Illustration. A, an accomplice, gives an account of a robbery in which he took part. He describes various incidents unconnected with the robbery which occurred on his way to and from the place where it was committed. Independent evidence of these facts may be given in order to corroborate his evidence as to the robbery itself.
summary: A witness whose testimony is to be corroborated may be asked about other circumstances observed at or near the time of the relevant fact, provided the court believes such details would support the testimony.
ingredients: Witness may be questioned about other circumstances observed at or near the time of the relevant fact
Court must be of the opinion that such circumstances would corroborate the witness’s testimony
explanation: Illustration. A, an accomplice, gives an account of a robbery in which he took part. He describes various incidents unconnected with the robbery which occurred on his way to and from the place where it was committed. Independent evidence of these facts may be given in order to corroborate his evidence as to the robbery itself.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 159, Page 43

description: In order to corroborate the testimony of a witness, any former statement made by such witness relating to the same fact, at or about the time when the fact took place, or before any authority legally competent to investigate the fact, may be proved.
summary: A witness’s testimony may be corroborated by proving any earlier statement he made about the same fact, made at the time of the fact or before any competent investigating authority.
ingredients: Former statements of the witness relating to the same fact can be proved to corroborate testimony
Statement may be made at/about the time of the fact or before any competent investigating authority
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 160, Page 5

description: Whenever any statement, relevant under section 26 or 27, is proved, all matters may be proved either in order to contradict or to corroborate it, or in order to impeach or confirm the credit of the person by whom it was made, which might have been proved if that person had been called as a witness and had denied upon cross-examination the truth of the matter suggested.
summary: If a statement covered by sections 26 or 27 is proved, any matter may be proved to contradict, corroborate, impeach, or confirm the credibility of the person who made it, as if that person had been cross‑examined.
ingredients: All matters may be proved to contradict or corroborate a proved statement under s.26 or s.27
All matters may be proved to impeach or confirm the credit of the person who made the statement
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 161, Page 43

description: ( 1 ) A witness may, while under examination, refresh his memory by referring to any writing made by himself at the time of the transaction concerning which he is questioned, or so soon afterwards that the Court considers it likely that the transaction was at that time fresh in his memory: Provided that the witness may also refer to any such writing made by any other person, and read by the witness within the time aforesaid, if when he read it, he knew it to be correct. ( 2 ) Whenever a witness may refresh his memory by reference to any document, he may, with the permission of the Court, refer to a copy of such document: Provided that the Court be satisfied that there is sufficient reason for the non-production of the original: Provided further that an expert may refresh his memory by reference to professional treatises.
summary: A witness may refresh his memory by referring to his own or another’s writing made at or shortly after the relevant transaction, and may also refer to a copy of a document with court permission, provided reasons for non‑production of the original are satisfactory; experts may use professional treatises.
ingredients: Witness may refer to his own writing made at the time of the transaction or shortly thereafter
Witness may refer to writing made by another person if he read it and knew it to be correct
With court permission, witness may refer to a copy of a document if there is sufficient reason for non‑production of the original
An expert may refresh memory by reference to professional treatises
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 162, Page 43

description: A witness may also testify to facts mentioned in any such document as is mentioned in section 162, although he has no specific recollection of the facts themselves, if he is sure that the facts were correctly recorded in the document.
summary: A witness may testify to facts recorded in a document referred to in section 162 even without personal recollection, provided he is confident the document records the facts correctly.
ingredients: Witness may testify to facts in a document mentioned in section 162 without personal recollection
Testimony is permissible if the witness is sure the facts were correctly recorded
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 163, Page 43

description: Any writing referred to under the provisions of the two last preceding sections shall be produced and shown to the adverse party if he requires it; such party may, if he pleases, cross-examine the witness thereupon.
summary: A party who requests a writing must have it produced and shown; the requesting party may cross‑examine the witness about that writing.
ingredients: Writing must be produced if the adverse party requires it
Adverse party may cross‑examine the witness on the writing
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 164, Page 44

description: (1) A witness summoned to produce a document shall, if it is in his possession or power, bring it to Court, notwithstanding any objection which there may be to its production or to its admissibility: Provided that the validity of any such objection shall be decided on by the Court. (2) The Court, if it sees fit, may inspect the document, unless it refers to matters of State, or take other evidence to enable it to determine on its admissibility. (3) If for such a purpose it is necessary to cause any document to be translated, the Court may, if it thinks fit, direct the translator to keep the contents secret, unless the document is to be given in evidence and, if the interpreter disobeys such direction, he shall be held to have committed an offence under section 198 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023: Provided that no Court shall require any communication between the Ministers and the President of India to be produced before it.
summary: A summoned witness must produce a document in his possession despite objections, which the Court will decide. The Court may inspect the document, subject to State matters, and can order secret translation, penalising non‑compliance.
ingredients: Witness must bring the document to Court if in his possession or power
Objections to production or admissibility are decided by the Court
Court may inspect the document unless it concerns State matters
Court may order secret translation of the document
Translator who disobeys a secret‑translation direction commits an offence under section 198 BNS
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 165, Page 44

description: When a party calls for a document which he has given the other party notice to produce, and such document is produced and inspected by the party calling for its production, he is bound to give it as evidence if the party producing it requires him to do so.
summary: If a party requests a document that the other party has been notified to produce and the document is produced and inspected, the requesting party must admit it as evidence if the producing party so demands.
ingredients: Notice must have been given to produce the document
Document is produced and inspected by the requesting party
Requesting party is bound to admit it as evidence if the producing party requires
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 166, Page 44

description: When a party refuses to produce a document which he has had notice to produce, he cannot afterwards use the document as evidence without the consent of the other party or the order of the Court. Illustration. Asues B on an agreement and gives B notice to produce it. At the trial, A calls for the document and B refuses to produce it. A gives secondary evidence of its contents. B seeks to produce the document itself to contradict the secondary evidence given by A, or in order to show that the agreement is not stamped. He cannot do so.
summary: A party who declines to produce a noticed document cannot later rely on that document as evidence unless the other party consents or the Court orders it.
ingredients: Refusal to produce a noticed document bars later use of that document as evidence
Consent of the other party or Court order is required to use the document later
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 167, Page 44

description: The Judge may, in order to discover or obtain proof of relevant facts, ask any question he considers necessary, in any form, at any time, of any witness, or of the parties about any fact; and may order the production of any document or thing; and neither the parties nor their representatives shall be entitled to make any objection to any such question or order, nor, without the leave of the Court, to cross‑examine any witness upon any answer given in reply to any such question: Provided that the judgment must be based upon facts declared by this Adhiniyam to be relevant, and duly proved: Provided further that this section shall not authorise any Judge to compel any witness to answer any question, or to produce any document which such witness would be entitled to refuse to answer or produce under sections 127 to 136, both inclusive, if the question were asked or the document were called for by the adverse party; nor shall the Judge ask any question which it would be improper for any other person to ask under section 151 or 152; nor shall he dispense with primary evidence of any document, except in the cases hereinbefore excepted.
summary: A Judge can ask any necessary question of any witness or party and order production of documents, without objection, but cannot compel answers or documents that the witness is lawfully entitled to refuse under sections 127‑136, nor ask improper questions under sections 151‑152, and must not dispense with primary evidence except as exempted.
ingredients: Judge may ask any necessary question of any witness or party
Judge may order production of any document or thing
No objection to such questions or orders is permitted
Cross‑examination of answers requires Court leave
Judgment must be based on relevant, duly proved facts
Judge cannot compel answers or documents that witness may refuse under sections 127‑136
Judge cannot ask questions improper under sections 151‑152
Primary evidence of a document cannot be dispensed with except as exempted
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 168, Page 44

description: The improper admission or rejection of evidence shall not be ground of itself for a new trial or reversal of any decision in any case, if it shall appear to the Court before which such objection is raised that, independently of the evidence objected to and admitted, there was sufficient evidence to justify the decision, or that, if the rejected evidence had been received, it ought not to have varied the decision.
summary: Improper admission or rejection of evidence does not by itself merit a new trial or reversal if the Court finds that, apart from the contested evidence, there was enough evidence to support the decision, or that the rejected evidence would not have changed the outcome.
ingredients: Improper admission/rejection is not a ground for new trial if sufficient other evidence exists
Court must assess whether the decision would have changed had the rejected evidence been admitted
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 169, Page 45

description: (1) The Indian Evidence Act, 1872 is hereby repealed. (2) Notwithstanding such repeal, if, immediately before the date on which this Adhiniyam comes into force, there is any application, trial, inquiry, investigation, proceeding or appeal pending, then, such application, trial, inquiry, investigation, proceeding or appeal shall be dealt with under the provisions of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872, as in force immediately before such commencement, as if this Adhiniyam had not come into force.
summary: The Indian Evidence Act, 1872 is repealed, but any pending proceedings as of the commencement of this Act will continue to be governed by the 1872 Act as if the new Act were not in force.
ingredients: Repeal of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872
Pending proceedings before commencement are to be governed by the 1872 Act
sourceReference: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (No. 47 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 170, Page 45
